hero.headline: Massachusetts worker classification: the ABC test explained
hero.subhead: Three prongs. All required. No exemptions.

partner_badge.text: Served by Teamed partner network: Suna Solutions, Inc. (primary)

capsule.label: Answer.cite this
capsule.text: Massachusetts presumes every worker is an employee. To classify someone as an independent contractor, you must satisfy all three prongs of the ABC test under M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B — and failing one prong alone means the worker is an employee by law.

sections.heading: What is the Massachusetts ABC test?
sections.answer_first: The ABC test under M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B presumes every worker is an employee. You must prove all three prongs to classify someone as an independent contractor — and there are no exemptions.
sections.body:
Massachusetts law starts with a presumption against contractor status. Unlike federal common-law tests, which weigh multiple factors, the ABC test is conjunctive: all three prongs must be satisfied. A single failure means the worker is an employee, regardless of what the contract says.

The three prongs are:

  • Prong A (Control): The worker is free from control and direction in connection with the performance of the service, both under the contract and in fact.
  • Prong B (Scope of business): The service is performed outside the usual course of the business of the employer.
  • Prong C (Independent business): The worker is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, profession, or business of the same nature as that involved in the service performed.

The presumption of employment is statutory. The burden of proof sits with the employer, not the worker. Your contracts don't change that burden.

sections.heading: Why does Prong B trip up most tech and professional services companies?
sections.answer_first: Prong B requires that the work be performed outside the usual course of the employer's business. A tech company engaging a software engineer for core product work fails this prong by default.
sections.body:
Prong B is the practical barrier that makes Massachusetts one of the strictest classification states in the US. Think of it this way: if you run a software company and you bring in a contractor to write software, that work is squarely inside your usual course of business. The contractor fails Prong B and is legally an employee.

The same logic applies across professional services:

  • A marketing agency engaging a graphic designer for client campaigns — same business, Prong B fails.
  • A law firm using a contract attorney for client matters — same practice, Prong B fails.
  • A staffing agency placing workers in the same role the agency provides to its clients — same business, Prong B fails.

The safe Prong B case is a plumber hired by a restaurant to fix a pipe — entirely outside the restaurant's usual course of business. That narrow exception rarely applies in knowledge-work sectors.

In Sebago v. Boston Cab Dispatch, Inc., 471 Mass. 321 (2015), the Supreme Judicial Court found that taxicab drivers satisfied all three prongs because the radio association's usual course of business was dispatch, not driving — one of the few published cases where Prong B was cleared in a service-sector context.

sections.heading: What are the penalties for misclassification in Massachusetts?
sections.answer_first: Treble damages — triple the back wages owed — are mandatory under M.G.L. c. 149, § 150. The court has no discretion to reduce them. Add attorney fees, fines up to $25,000, and criminal liability for wilful violations.
sections.body:
The financial exposure from a Massachusetts misclassification finding is severe and non-negotiable:

  • Treble damages (mandatory): Section 150 requires the court to award three times the unpaid wages and benefits. There is no judicial discretion here — unlike federal liquidated damages, which courts can reduce, Massachusetts treble damages are automatic once liability is established.
  • Attorney fees: The employer pays the worker's legal costs on top of the treble damages.
  • Civil fines: Up to $25,000 per violation.
  • Criminal liability (wilful): Up to $25,000 fine and one year in prison for a first offence. Corporate officers and agents carry personal liability — the corporate shield does not protect individuals who directed the misclassification.
  • Criminal liability (non-wilful): Up to $10,000 and six months' imprisonment for a first offence.

In Patel v. 7-Eleven, Inc., 489 Mass. 356 (2022), the Supreme Judicial Court confirmed that the ABC test applies to franchise relationships with no federal preemption, extending the reach of § 148B beyond traditional employment arrangements.

The practical implication: a misclassified contractor earning $80,000/year for three years represents a potential liability of $720,000 in back wages alone before trebling. You don't negotiate that down.

sections.heading: Are there any exemptions to the Massachusetts ABC test?
sections.answer_first: No. Massachusetts § 148B has no industry-specific exemption pathways. Every three prongs must be satisfied for every worker in every industry.
sections.body:
California's AB5 — the other benchmark for strict ABC-test states — built in a list of profession-specific exemptions for doctors, lawyers, architects, and others. Massachusetts did not. Section 148B applies uniformly across all industries and all worker types with no carve-outs.

This is the structural reason why the Massachusetts classification risk is qualitatively different from other US states. In California, a company can argue a licensed professional fits an exemption. In Massachusetts, that argument doesn't exist.

What this means for employers building teams in Massachusetts:

  • No safe-harbour certifications that convert contractor relationships to compliant ones.
  • No industry guild exceptions that protect your sector.
  • No grandfather clauses for pre-existing contractor arrangements.

An Employer of Record arrangement removes the classification question entirely. You retain day-to-day management of the worker's tasks; Teamed acts as the legal employer, runs payroll, files state taxes, and handles PFML contributions. No Prong B analysis required because there's no contractor relationship to classify.

sections.heading: How does an Employer of Record remove Massachusetts classification risk?
sections.answer_first: An EOR makes the worker a statutory employee from day one. You direct their work; the EOR is the legal employer. No ABC test applies because there's no contractor relationship.
sections.body:
The Teamed EOR model in Massachusetts works through Suna Solutions, Inc. — Teamed's primary US partner. The worker goes on Suna's payroll compliantly from hire date. You manage their day-to-day activities; Teamed handles:

  • Massachusetts payroll tax withholding (5% flat rate + any surtax withholding obligations)
  • UI contributions under Schedule E (2.42% new employer rate on the $15,000 wage base)
  • PFML contributions (0.42% employer share for employers of 25+)
  • Earned sick time administration under M.G.L. c. 149, § 148C (1 hour per 30 worked, up to 40 hours/year)
  • Workers' compensation coverage

The Teamed fee is $599 per employee per month, flat. Zero FX mark-up if you pay in a currency other than USD. Statutory contributions — payroll tax, UI, PFML — pass through at cost on every invoice, itemised so you see every line.

If your Massachusetts headcount reaches the point where setting up a US entity makes sense, a named Teamed specialist runs that transition through the graduation model. You move from EOR to your own entity on one platform with no redundant contracts or parallel systems. That's the crossover point — a real human walks you through it, not a bot.

sections.heading: What should you know before engaging workers in Massachusetts?
sections.body: Massachusetts enforcement isn't passive. The Attorney General's Fair Labor Division has independent enforcement authority — it doesn't need a worker complaint to open an investigation. AG-initiated audits of contractor-heavy industries (tech, delivery, construction, gig platforms) are active. And unlike wage theft in some states, Massachusetts misclassification carries personal liability for corporate officers who directed the arrangement. EOR isn't a compliance checkbox in Massachusetts; it's the structural answer to a law that was designed to make contractor classification difficult. The enforcement record shows it works as designed.

faq_schema.q: What is the Massachusetts ABC test for worker classification?
faq_schema.a: The ABC test under M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B presumes every worker is an employee. To classify someone as an independent contractor, the employer must prove all three prongs: (A) free from control, (B) service outside usual course of business, and (C) engaged in an independent trade or business.

faq_schema.q: What are the penalties for misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor in Massachusetts?
faq_schema.a: Treble damages (mandatory 3x back wages) under M.G.L. c. 149, § 150, plus attorney fees, civil fines up to $25,000, and criminal liability for wilful violations including up to one year in prison for corporate officers.

faq_schema.q: Can a tech company use independent contractors in Massachusetts?
faq_schema.a: Generally no. Prong B of the ABC test requires the work to be outside the employer's usual course of business. A tech company engaging software engineers for core product work fails Prong B, making the workers employees by law.

faq_schema.q: Are there exemptions to the Massachusetts ABC test?
faq_schema.a: No. Unlike California's AB5, Massachusetts M.G.L. c. 149, § 148B has no industry-specific exemption pathways. All three prongs must be satisfied for every worker in every industry.

faq_schema.q: How does an Employer of Record help with Massachusetts worker classification?
faq_schema.a: An EOR makes the worker a statutory employee from day one, eliminating the need for any ABC test analysis. The EOR handles payroll, tax withholding, PFML contributions, and compliance. No misclassification risk because there is no contractor relationship.
